data.title: FLSA: Preferable To KSA, But Not A Good Time To Pursue
data.description:
Introduction

The Franklin FTSE Saudi Arabia ETF (NYSEARCA:FLSA), which provides focussed access to Saudi Arabian equities (of which over two-thirds of the portfolio are giant caps and large-caps), hasn’t had the sunniest start to the year. While there is some comfort in seeing it outperform global stocks by around 447bps, it hasn’t quite managed to fare better than other emerging market equities that are almost flat YTD (on average). Incidentally, all three pockets are in the red this year.

Is there scope for Saudi equities to get going, and should FLSA be the preferred vehicle to play this theme? Let’s start by addressing the latter.

The Drawbacks and Merits of Pursuing FLSA over KSA

Besides FLSA, investors who want an ETF-type vehicle to access Saudi equities also have the option of pursuing the iShares MSCI Saudi Arabia ETF (KSA) which tracks a different index (the MSCI Saudi Arabia IMI 25/50 Index) to the one that FLSA tracks (FTSE Saudi Arabia Capped Index). Now, KSA has been in existence for a longer period that FLSA (a three-year head start), but we still feel quite perplexed about the vast differential in the AUM of these two products (with over $680m in AUM, KSA is around 40x larger than our focus ETF); one would think that KSA has a lot of things going for it, but barring a few minor flaws, we don’t see any major reason why investors should shun FLSA and go with KSA.

One reason why investors may be gravitating to KSA is that it is a lot wider in reach, covering over 133 Saudi stocks, almost twice as much as the stocks that FLSA covers (less than 70). Given that we’re dealing with a terrain with quite high idiosyncratic risks, and close correlation to the vagaries of oil prices, it may make sense to spread the net across a wider pool of stocks. KSA also offers almost thrice the exposure of FLSA to small-cap stocks that are considered to have a more attractive earnings growth runway. However, note that from a volatility perspective, it doesn’t really make a big difference, with KSA currently having a slightly higher risk profile to FLSA.

The other area where FLSA falls short is on the liquidity front, which may put off those who want to make short-term tactical bets. The dollar volumes seen per day only equate to around $25,000 per day, whereas in KSA’s case it is a lot more impressive at $21m. Needless to say, this results in much tighter spreads for KSA (the difference between the bid and the ask equates to just 0.04%) whereas in FLSA’s case it is over 5-6x worse.

The final facet where KSA trumps FLSA is on the income front. Traditionally (over the last 4 years), FLSA has maintained a slightly superior yield of over 20bps, but over the past 3 years, KSA has grown its dividends at over 31%, a much superior pace to FLSA (17%) resulting in a better current yield of almost 3.5% (FLSA’s current yield is a little over 3%).

With regard to all the other aspects (and many would view these factors as more pertinent), it is FLSA which comes out on top. Firstly, there’s the superior cost efficiency that FLSA offers, with an expense ratio of 0.39%, which is roughly only half as much as what KSA costs (0.75%). Then, despite following a much smaller pool of names, FLSA is a more stable portfolio and doesn’t resort to a great deal of churn on an annual basis (only 15% of its holdings get turned over every year, half as much as what KSA resorts to).

Investors are also prepared to overlook quite a few flaws if a product can deliver the goods in terms of returns, and here it is FLSA which truly flourishes.

Note that since its inception it has consistently outperformed KSA with the current differential working out to over 1200bps. It isn’t just the total return profile that’s worth highlighting.

For the total risk that both these products take, FLSA delivers better excess returns (as measured by a superior Sharpe ratio). Excess return capabilities only in the face of downside deviation is even more impressive, with FLSA beating KSA on the Sortino ratio front as well.

All in all, if one is willing to overlook the poor liquidity of FLSA, we think it should be the preferred route to play Saudi equities.

Is FLSA A Good Buy Now?

As noted in the previous section, given a choice between FLSA and KSA, we would plump for the former, but that doesn’t necessarily make our focus ETF a good buy now.

Over the years, while Saudi has been taking steps to dilute the contribution of oil to its overall prospects, one still can’t help but think that the black gold still serves as its chief catalyst. With the export of oil accounting for around 90% of government revenue, and almost 50% of its GDP coming from oil and gas activity, it is difficult to turn a blind eye to the dimming prospect of higher oil prices.

What we find particularly concerning is that 8 OPEC+ members have now decided to increase oil output in May by over 3x from the initial expected spike of just, 135000 barrels per day [bpd]. We are not sure this is the most sensible plan of action when global oil demand for 2025 has already recently been curtailed significantly by around 60% from previous estimates of, 730000 bpd to just 300000 bpd. Note that oil price forecasts have taken quite a beating in recent weeks compared to what was expected before.

If oil prices don’t stage a meaningful recovery and continue to hover at low levels, this will likely dampen demand for credit growth, which would be a negative for FLSA’s dominant stocks (over 40% of the portfolio consists of banks). Looking at the brighter side, some may see a potential slowdown in credit growth as healthy, given that the liquidity position of Saudi banks isn’t the most resilient, with the loan to deposit ratio trending above 100% levels, and shifting the onus to external funding.

Our reluctance to pursue FLSA is currently also dictated by the total loss of momentum seen in this counter. Note that on the daily chart, FLSA has dropped below all its three key moving averages (50,100, and 200) in April.

Despite the recent weakness, FLSA’s relative strength [RS] ratio versus the broader emerging market pack is still at a relatively high threshold, with a current figure that is 10% above average. This may prompt rotational specialists to move out of FLSA into cheaper emerging markets. For context, FLSA’s current valuations are hardly cheap either, trading at a forward P/E of almost 18x, which represents a massive 43% premium over the corresponding multiple of emerging markets.

data.title: Jackson Financial: High Beta, High Yield
data.description:
Introduction

Jackson Financial (NYSE:JXN) might be one of the most complex and misunderstood stocks I’ve looked at in a while. It offers a dividend yield north of 4%, trades at a single-digit earnings multiple, and has outperformed the S&P 500 since its spin-off. As we will explore in this article, the company also moves like a true high-beta small-cap stock—volatile, erratic, and highly sensitive to interest rates and market sentiment. Given that the company is in the annuity and insurance business, it is especially volatile in financial conditions. While this presents some risks, it also presents the opportunity, which I would like to explore today.

In this article, I break down the structure of the business, highlight the reasons for its seemingly cheap valuation, and explore whether JXN deserves a place in income-focused portfolios—or whether the volatility and business model risks are too significant to ignore.

Company Overview

Jackson is a U.S. focused annuity provider, spun off from UK-based Prudential in 2021 (PUK). The business is centered around retirement income planning, primarily through fixed and variable annuity contracts. These products promise long-term, often guaranteed payouts to policyholders and are typically marketed as a way to secure lifetime income in retirement.

To support these obligations, Jackson manages a large portfolio of investments both directly and in client-owned funds, over $330 billion, with fixed income securities comprising roughly half of this total. The investment spread (the difference between what Jackson earns on its investments and what it pays out to annuity holders) is a key driver of profitability, along with fees earned on assets under management. Given the revenue model of the company, it is easy to understand why it is highly sensitive to financial news and macroeconomic events, particularly pertaining to interest rate fluctuations.

The company's balance sheet is quite complex and took me quite a bit of time to understand at a fundamental enough level to be able to analyze. I will do my best to simplify it for the purposes of the article. The main thing to understand is that the company has multiple buckets of capital, mainly bifurcated between "general account assets" and "separate account assets". Separate account assets are held on behalf of policyholders in variable annuity contracts, and are legally segregated from the parent company's assets and liabilities. General account assets, on the other hand, are managed directly by Jackson and used to back fixed annuities, guaranteed benefits, and surplus capital. The company bears investment risk on general account assets but not on separate account assets which are passed through to policyholders.

The company earns revenue primarily from fees generated by separate account assets, similar to how mutual fund managers collect management fees, and from investment spread on general account assets, defined as the difference between investment income and the liabilities owed to policyholders.

Investment Thesis: Levered Beta on Financial Markets

Given that the economics of the company are almost exclusively driven by financial asset values and capital flows, it is easy to see how the role a company like this might play in a portfolio. The underlying business economics function somewhat like highly levered beta on the equity and bond markets. Since >99% of the general account investments are held in fixed-income or other debt instruments like commercial mortgages and the parent company bears investment risk on these assets, the company's spread is exposed to interest rate fluctuations. In the separate account fee base, the company has more equity exposure, as more than 75% of the separate account assets are equities.

Said more simply, the company earns more fees when markets are up and their spread economics can change quite a bit when interest rates move. This gets interesting when looked at through the lens of how interest rate fluctuations impact the demand for annuities. Since annuities are yield-like instruments, in theory, they should be in more demand when interest rates go up. Jackson can also take the annuity sales and invest them at higher spreads in a rising interest rate environment. Of course, this also means that the book value of their assets will decrease in a rising rate regime. Indeed, the company reported that the market/book value of their general investment portfolio is .93.

So, how does this translate to a coherent investment thesis? Well, in a portfolio like mine that is yield-focused and low-beta, it does pay to have a few tickers that are high beta to still capture upside in bull markets. JXN fits this bill very cleanly, especially given its exposure to financial markets. The thing that makes JXN especially attractive in addition is that it is currently trading at a very strong dividend yield of ~4.5%, yet has increased dividends 60% since spin-off. This is largely due to absurdly large buybacks since the spin-off, in which outstanding shares have decreased by 18%.

The fact that I can augment the beta of my portfolio through the convexity of a position that approximates the 10-year treasury in yield is very interesting. Even more interesting is that the yield on cost for these shares could increase pretty significantly for the next several years, although the long-term viability of this name as a dividend compounder is a bit more obscure.

The second element driving an investment thesis here is that I see the economics of the underlying business being an interesting play on rising rates. While rising rates do harm book value of already held fixed income, they increase the demand for annuities and allow Jackson to continue investing at strong spreads. Since a structural rise in rates over time might depress equity markets, this presents the risk of lots of volatility along the way.

It's Not as "Cheap" as it Looks, but Still Cheap

Jackson trades at under 4x earnings and yields more than 4%, with modest earnings growth projected going forward. The valuation screens attractively on virtually every metric—P/E, P/B, dividend yield, and free cash flow. The company generated $767M of free cash flow in 2024, which is roughly 15% FCF yield at today's price in the mid $70/share range. This is indeed an eye-popping level of yield and should be attractive to deep value investors looking for bargains.

I think it is appropriate for a no-moat financials business with strong market risks to trade at less than 10x whatever metric you can conceive of, so in that light, it is probably not reasonable to expect the business to rerate substantially from there. However, the company's valuation is still absurdly low, especially when taking a look at book value. At around $5B of market cap, the company trades at roughly half the book value of the current account assets and around 1x 2024 revenue.

Volatility and Net Flows

Since its spin-off, JXN has been a volatile stock, and part of that reflects the complexity of its net flow dynamics. While headline figures show ongoing net outflows, particularly from traditional variable annuities, which saw $18.6 billion in net withdrawals in 2024, those numbers reflect the natural runoff of a mature block rather than deterioration in franchise value. Many of these policies were written during the peak VA cycle a decade ago and are now beyond surrender periods, making elevated lapses and withdrawals both expected and manageable. Jackson is actively repositioning its business: Registered index-linked annuities (RILAs) delivered $5.5 billion in net inflows in 2024, and fixed annuities added another $1.45 billion, signaling that Jackson is not just replacing old assets, but growing in high-demand product segments. Importantly, total retail annuity account values rose 6.9% year-over-year, supported by market appreciation, which in turn lifts the company’s all-important fee revenue base.

Management’s long-term goal isn’t simply to flip net flows positive, but to rebalance the business mix to more capital-efficient, growth-oriented products while returning excess capital to shareholders. In that regard, 2024 was a step forward: the company hit record RILA sales, expanded its advisory channel footprint, and grew its overall annuity footprint to $266.7 billion in gross account value, up from $253.8 billion the prior year. If RILA growth persists and VA outflows normalize, as seems likely given the aging cohort of redemptions, Jackson could begin generating net inflows on a consolidated basis by mid-to-late 2025. This would mark a meaningful inflection point: rather than simply managing runoff, Jackson would be growing new, recurring revenue streams while monetizing legacy capital. For investors, the payoff could be a modest re-rating of the stock’s valuation multiple.

Generous Shareholder Returns

As mentioned previously, Jackson has returned significant amounts of capital to shareholders. I noted that the company has returned $1.8B to investors since spin-off. Given that the company started trading at a $3B market cap, this means that 60% of the entire starting market cap has been returned to shareholders in three and a half years. Looked at through this lens, it shouldn't be much of a surprise that the stock has crushed the market in that timeframe.

Management is targeting $700-$800M of capital return to shareholders for 2025, which would be a large increase from 2024. The quarterly dividend recently being raised 14% from $0.70 to $0.80 indicates confidence from management in hitting their target for the upcoming year. What makes this especially interesting is that shares have drawn down roughly 35% from their all-time high, making buybacks at the current price an even stronger driver of shareholder returns.

For investors focused on yield, this makes JXN an interesting candidate. However, the sustainability of that dividend is tied closely to investment performance and realized returns on the underlying portfolio. Any material deterioration in spreads, or sharp equity market drawdowns, could impact capital available for distribution. My comments there are mainly regarding the outlook over the long run. For the year ahead, the company is very well-capitalized, having ended 2024 at a 527% statutory capital position, far above the mandated 100% and the company's own target of 425%.

Risks

The risks associated with Jackson Financial are meaningful and shouldn’t be glossed over. First, the company is highly sensitive to financial market conditions, as we previously discussed. This sensitivity adds a layer of volatility that may not be immediately obvious to investors attracted by surface-level valuation metrics. This dynamic is also what creates the potential opportunity in the shares. The fact that JXN shares consistently have a volatility that is 10% higher than the S&P 500 is a sword that cuts both ways, and investors should expect it to behave with the trading behavior of levered beta.

Competition in the annuity space also presents a risk. Many of the products Jackson offers are relatively commoditized, and the company competes with larger, more diversified insurers that may enjoy better distribution networks, brand recognition, or lower costs. In other words, annuity providers like Jackson don't have a moat and must compete on fees and their track record of investment performance, which can be challenging.

Lastly, net outflows are something to be watched. Ongoing net outflows can weigh on fee income and dampen operating leverage. Reversing this trend through continued strong growth in RILA sales will be necessary for Jackson to command a higher multiple over time. Right now, the market does seem to feel comfortable that the trend is going in the right direction, but each earnings report should be scrutinized when it comes to net flows.

Technical Analysis

The stock is currently in a downtrend, making lower highs and lower lows. As long as the macroeconomic picture remains tenuous, I expect the stock to trade poorly. That being said, the point of holding "levered beta" in this name is to hit a bounce off lows with greater force than the broader market, while collecting strong yield. I would urge investors to think twice before accumulating shares along the downtrend resistance line.

Final Thoughts: Initiating at Buy, But Not for Everyone

Jackson Financial is a unique stock. On paper, it looks incredibly cheap. And for income investors who understand the business model and can stomach some volatility, it may offer an attractive mix of yield and total return potential. Investors should expect this name to drawdown sharply when the large cap indices are selling off.

It’s a highly "leveraged" annuity business with complex financial statements and sensitivity to macro conditions. For that reason, I think JXN belongs on the watchlist for value-oriented income investors, but position sizing, timing, and a strong grasp of the balance sheet are critical. I have sized into a 1% position in my portfolio because my low-beta income strategy can benefit from more volatile tickers with attractive total return prospects. That being said, the macro outlook for the stock market at large is somewhat challenging, so I expect volatility in the years ahead. I do think that with the current yield approximating the 10-year treasury though that the stock offers enough in income return alone to be able to take a flier at the current valuation.

data.title: 2 Great REITs At Big Discount, Magnificent Gain Potential
data.description:
Co-authored by Treading Softly.

"La-La-La LAVA! Ch-ch-ch CHICKEN!"

– Steve

One side of the news cycle has been completely obsessed with everything going on with the current presidential administration. The other side of the news cycle has been bewildered by the popularity of one specific film. It's not common for a Jack Black film to escalate to the popularity we've seen with the recent Minecraft movie – which has demolished films put out by giants like Walt Disney (DIS), with its Snow White adaptation. Yet, the formula behind the Minecraft movie is also bewildering movie critics. The movie, widely panned by critics as being uninfluential, uninspired, and potentially boring, has turned into a phenomenal success for Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD). The Minecraft movie has become one of the most successful openings for Warner Bros. – ever.

Have you ever wondered how you can benefit from an unusual phenomenon of this type? For a long time, people have been claiming that the movie theater business will die, and yet it is still a persistent force within our culture. There's something about observing a movie in a dark room with a massive screen and speaker system you can't easily have at home and enjoying it with others. When I see something of this nature make ripples across our society, I wonder to myself if there is a way that I can benefit from the unexpected success of certain films. The answer is, you can.

It is common that people will be more willing to spend money on experiences than they are to spend on "stuff." We live in a very commercial society where people want to gather a lot of things, but there has been a rising push in recent years towards spending more money on an experience that will last with you forever than something that may come from China and suddenly face massive tariffs.

Today, I want to highlight two great ways you can gain income from experientially focused companies.

Let's dive in!

Long Live Experiences

EPR Properties (EPR), yielding 7.3%, is an equity REIT that focuses on "experiential" properties. These are properties where people go to do things.

Originally, EPR started as a REIT for movie theaters, but has since diversified to include tenants like Top Golf, water parks, ski resorts, museums, specialty lodging, and a casino. Source.

Notably, these are industries that are not likely to be directly impacted by tariffs. A recession could have some impact, but typically, consumers tend to prioritize their entertainment spending. For example, movie revenue has traditionally gone up during recessions:

When it comes to recreation, the collapse during COVID was an exception, not the rule: Source.

EPR's space was the hardest hit by COVID, but it has been recovering. We do not expect that trade wars or even a recession will threaten the ongoing recovery.

We expect EPR will continue to grow its portfolio at a methodical pace, and that means we can expect the dividend to continue growing.

Bet On The House's Income

VICI Properties Inc. (VICI), yielding 5.4%, is a property REIT dialed into specific styles of experiences. VICI came to life as a landlord of casinos, primarily of Caesars Entertainment (CZR) and later on with MGM Resorts (MGM). While VICI has continued to expand its portfolio into more than just casinos, the heavy focus remains on experiences.

Interest in gambling and the experience of traveling and going to a casino remains extremely high and extremely unimpacted by tariff activity. Even through economic cycles with a recession on the horizon, gambling activity continues to grow, and interest in gambling continues to be a large straw to the Las Vegas region.

VICI has been a very consistent and strong dividend growth option, and while the yield remains in the mid-level versus high-yield territory, it also has a stronger history of dividend growth than EPR does. This means that you can have one great option to be able to grow your income more rapidly in VICI and a higher-yielding option with the future dividend growth expected as well in EPR.

Conclusion

With EPR and VICI, you can enjoy strong recurrent income from the experiential side of consumer spending. Instead of trying to support an economy where you work hard to spend your money and buy things that potentially you don't need or that will simply fall apart over time, you can enjoy the side of the economy that provides consumers with unique and thrilling experiences that they will be talking about for the rest of their lives.

At the end of the day, having exposure to both sides of the economy can provide you with a strong and stable income for your retirement portfolio. Just because you're retired does not mean that you will disengage from the economy altogether. You're going to be engaged in daily spending, whether that's buying food at the grocery store, filling up your gas tank at the gas station, buying goods that you need to enjoy life, or paying for experiences.

The question I have for you is, why shouldn't you also be paid by all those exact same sources? If you unlock truly passive income for the market and from the economy by tapping into the different ways that you spend money and recognizing that others are doing the exact same thing, you can then unlock a lifetime of recurrent passive income that can flood your expenses, overwhelm your reservoir and provide you with an abundant income to be able to enjoy. Stop just collecting money to be able to spend it later and see it disappear into the ether; instead, build up a portfolio that will provide you with income from what other people are spending and be able to enjoy a retirement of relaxation and calm.

That's the beauty of my Income Method. That's the beauty of income investing.

data.title: If I Retired Today, These 3 Dividend Stocks Would Be My Paycheck
data.description:
Introduction

Sometimes, going for safety doesn't work out.

In this case, I'm talking about a typical 60/40 portfolio, which is one of the most popular portfolio constructions with a balance of risk between stocks (60%) and bonds (40%).

Technically speaking, about 90% of the portfolio's risk comes from the equity side, which provides a very favorable risk profile for a wide range of investors.

After all, when economic and/or market turmoil hits, we can assume the flight to safety will benefit bond prices, providing the 60/40 portfolio with downside protection.

So far, this has worked, as 60/40 investors haven't lost money during a single five-year trailing period since 1950, as the data below shows. Or to put it differently, in order to make money on a 60/40 strategy, all you need is a time horizon of at least five years. Technically speaking, of course.

One of the biggest benefits of this is the limited downside. Especially older investors who may have to sell assets to generate liquidity depend on assets that do not regularly sell off 30-40%. The same goes for other investors who may want to sell assets for whatever reason.

Now comes the bad news.

During the 2009-2021 period, the 60/40 portfolio was a fantastic asset, as both stock and bond prices were in a strong uptrend. Both benefited from subdued inflation, decent economic growth, and various support programs from the Federal Reserve and other major central banks.

That party has ended.

As Apollo Global Management data shows, the 60/40 portfolio has basically been unchanged since the start of 2022, returning just 2% per year over the past three and a half years, as the chart below shows.

Due to persistent inflation and budget uncertainty in the United States, bonds have lost their appeal, turning 40% of the 60/40 into somewhat of a liability instead of a safe haven.

Although nobody can predict the future, including me, the risks here are that prolonged sticky inflation and elevated rates turn the 60/40 portfolio into a laggard, which provides little to no capital gains and most of its income from bonds, which are now much more volatile than before.

As we can see below, the Merrill Lynch Options Volatility shows that bonds are much more volatile compared to the period before inflation became an issue.

Even worse, while inflation has come down substantially, new issues like elevated debt funding risks in the U.S. have caused another spike in volatility, as bonds did not become a safe haven during the latest turmoil.

Now what?

Before I continue, please note that I cannot give you personal advice. Everything I have ever written is food for thought, as it is impossible for me to address specific financial needs when writing articles for a big audience. I simply don't know your personal situation.

Having said all of this, I have never been a fan of bonds, as I prefer to buy equities in companies. Being an owner of great companies allows me to benefit from a company's success and entitles me to perpetual dividend payments.

Now, more than ever, I believe alternatives to bonds are important.

Hence, in this article, I'll present a few that come with super safe dividends, very stable business models to protect investors against mayhem, and valuations that give investors a shot at beating the market (and 60/40) investors over time.

In other words, I'll give you three bond-like stocks I would buy in a heartbeat if I were to de-risk my portfolio and focus more on income.

These aren't just three random stocks I came up with to give you something to read, but three companies I would trust to eventually replace my current income.

Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) - Top-Tier Midstream Income

It may not come as a surprise that EPD is part of this article, as I love the midstream industry. In January, I called it "My Single Favorite Place To Buy Income," as the industry benefits from secular growth like high energy production/demand, a bigger focus on free cash flow, and years of underwhelming supply growth, which comes with pricing tailwinds.

Enterprise Products Partners, which is structured as an MLP and issues a K-1 form, is an all-star in this industry, as it has grown its distribution (dividend) for 26 consecutive years. It currently yields 7.1% and comes with a five-year CAGR of 3.5%. This growth rate is not very high, but enough to protect income against moderate inflation.

This distribution has been covered by distributable cash flow for more than 20 years, with increasing payout safety in recent years, as cash flow has grown faster than the quarterly distribution.

Moreover, the company has an A-range credit rating and a 'mission-critical' business model, which comes with more than 50,000 miles of pipelines in all major energy-producing regions in the U.S.

It also has more than 300 million barrels of storage capacity, 20 deepwater docks, and a wide range of processing and gathering assets to support the production of natural gas liquids and other commodities.

This year, Enterprise Products Partners plans to add two more gas processing plants in the Permian, the Bahia NGL pipeline, Frac 14, and the first phase of its NGL export terminal on the Neches River.

Additional expansions include its ethane and ethylene terminal at Morgan's Point.

Essentially, these projects align with the company's goal to increase hydrocarbon exports, which reached 70 million barrels in December 2024, with a target of 100 million barrels per month by 2027.

For decades, the company has made the case that oil and gas are here to stay. It's a thesis I completely agree with, as I have written in countless articles.

The company also has one of the best track records of putting money to work, as it has an average return on invested capital of 13%. This is critical in this very capital-intensive industry.

It also needs to be said that EPD benefits from long-term demand growth without being exposed to commodity prices, as most of its income comes from take-or-pay and other fee-based contracts.

Valuation-wise, EPD trades at 7.9x operating cash flow, below its ten-year average of 9.6x. Per-unit operating cash flow is expected to grow by 4-8% per year through at least 2027. This paves the road for a $43 price target, more than 40% above its current price.

When adding its yield, I believe EPD offers a tremendous mix of income and growth.

The same goes for the next pick.

NNN REIT (NNN) - No-Drama Real Estate Income

One of the companies I often bring up in income-focused articles is Realty Income (O), the biggest net lease REIT in the world. Although I'm bullish on it, some investors have dumped the stock due to its exposure to drug stores and some struggling dollar store operators.

This gives its smaller peer, NNN REIT, an edge.

Please note that net lease means that tenants are responsible for taxes, maintenance, and insurance costs.

As we can see when looking at the NNN vs. O ratio in the chart below, NNN has outperformed O since 2021. I expect this trend to continue.

For starters, this smaller company has a focus on other tenants.

The majority of its nearly 3,600 properties are run by convenience store operators, automotive service providers, and restaurants (limited and full service). This includes 7-Eleven, Mister Car Wash, Dave & Buster's, Camping World, and many others. Not a single tenant accounts for more than 4.5% of its rent, which lowers concentration risks.

On top of having a weighted average remaining lease term of roughly ten years, the company has a fantastic track record, as more than 80% of its tenants tend to renew their leases. This is one of the reasons why the net lease REIT has consistently outperformed its peers when it comes to the average occupancy rate.

It ended last year with a 98.5% occupancy rate, a mile above the REIT industry average of 91.4% (as of 3Q24).

Moreover, while some tenants are struggling, the company is doing a great job of mitigating these risks. During its 4Q24 earnings call, the company mentioned that Badcock Furniture (32 properties) rejected all leases. However, NNN has already re-leased or sold 11 properties, achieving 113% rent recovery on those assets.

Meanwhile, Frisch's (64 properties) stopped paying rent, but 28 stores have been re-leased with a base rent of $2.8 million plus percentage rent.

Going forward, management expects minimal long-term financial impact (less than 1% per share) once all properties are resolved by 2026.

This stability is also visible in its dividend.

Currently yielding 5.7%, the dividend has been hiked for 35 consecutive years, with a CAGR in the single-digit range over the past five years.

The dividend is protected by a healthy payout ratio of 69% and a balance sheet with a BBB+ credit rating, which is one step below the A range.

With regard to its valuation, we see a favorable picture, as NNN trades at 12.1x adjusted funds from operations ("AFFO"), a mile below its ten-year average of 16.6x.

When including expectations of 3-4% annual per-share AFFO growth, we get a highly favorable total return outlook of at least 10% without any valuation gains. This is based on 3-4% annual AFFO growth on top of its dividend.

Moreover, while I am almost entirely certain (you never know for sure) that NNN won't suddenly turn into a high-flying stock, I believe it has what it takes to beat the market.

Especially if investors turn to value stocks, it has a good shot at generating outperforming returns, as it did between the early 2000s and 2015.

The next stock is very different.

CME Group (CME) - A Very Special Dividend Stock

CME Group is the one stock of this selection that I own, as it has more than a focus on income.

In fact, I like it so much that it was one of the three stocks presented in a recent article titled "Ultra High Conviction: My #1 Defensive Place To Invest Right Now."

CME Group is the world's largest exchange owner. Its portfolio includes the Chicago Mercantile Exchange (where it got its name), the Chicago Board of Trade, NYMEX, and COMEX.

On its exchanges, investors trade some of the most liquid futures in the world, including the S&P 500 e-mini. It also offers a wide range of agricultural, interest rate, energy, metal, and forex futures and options, providing investors and corporate players with everything they need to hedge risks and speculate.

This business model comes with a number of benefits.

  • Volatility becomes an investor's friend. The higher the volatility, the more money CME makes.
  • This is a high-margin business model, as it does not require a lot of capital to run. CME Group has the fourth-highest profit margin of the S&P 500.
  • Generally speaking, periods of elevated rates and inflation come with higher volatility. When adding the high-margin profile, CME is a business with tremendous inflation protection capabilities.

Moreover, the company is quickly expanding into high-growth areas like crypto, special materials, and international demand.

With regard to its dividend, the company usually returned 100% of its free cash flow to shareholders. For that, it uses four regular quarterly dividends and one special dividend. Initially, this special dividend was paid in the first quarter. Now, it will be paid in the last quarter.

Currently, I estimate an annualized dividend yield of 5%, with half of this coming from quarterly dividends. In February, the dividend was hiked by 8.7%.

While some may dislike that the regular quarterly dividend yield is not very high, I believe the big picture speaks for itself. I also like its valuation. Despite its strong performance this year, I see a path to >12% annual returns, as it trades roughly a point below its ten-year average P/E ratio with an annual EPS growth outlook of 6-7% through at least 2027.

If I were now fully focused on living off dividends, I would definitely own a lot more CME, as it comes with growth, income, inflation protection, and a special tailwind during periods of market fear.

Takeaway

The 60/40 portfolio isn't what it used to be.

Bonds, which were once the source of capital gains, income, and safety, have turned volatile, leaving investors with mediocre returns and limited downside protection, at least in this environment.

If you're like me, preferring ownership in resilient businesses over bond market unpredictability, there are better ways to generate income and stability.

Enterprise Products Partners offers a 7.1% yield, steady growth, and critical energy infrastructure. NNN REIT combines a 5.7% dividend with recession-resistant real estate and a 35-year payout growth streak. And CME Group thrives on volatility, delivering inflation-resistant income with a 5% yield and nearly unmatched margins.

None of these are bonds. But in today's market, they might just be better.

Risks To My Thesis

Persistent inflation or a deep recession could pressure EPD's energy demand, NNN's tenant health, or CME's trading volumes. Rising rates may also weigh on REIT valuations.

While these picks are defensive, no asset is immune to macro shocks.

data.title: Lam Research: Tides Have Turned, Buy This Semiconductor Refurbisher
data.description:
The semiconductor industry is arguably the biggest driver across the entire stock market. Outside of recent political news, semiconductors are at the forefront of investors' minds, research, and data. What makes these semiconductor devices actually operate? There are many pieces to the puzzle, but one big one is wafer fabrication. This is the process of turning semiconductor wafers into developed microchips through wafer fabrication equipment (WFE).

Lam Research Corp. (NASDAQ:LRCX) is a leading edge company on the front line of this WFE space. The firm designs, manufactures, refurbishes, and services semiconductor processing equipment used in the fabrication of integrated circuits. LRCX serves and has manufacturing in many parts of the world and is headquartered in California.

In the last 5 years, the stock is up 149% but has fallen heavily since its peak in last July. From $112 a share in mid-July to about $68 now in April, LRCX is down almost 40%. It's worth a look to see if rally time is here for the tech firm. I rate LRCX a buy. The semiconductor and wafer fab equipment industries are battling a volatile market at the moment but are poised for success and high demand well into the future. LRCX saw financial metrics fall in 2023, but they are back on the rise and are showing strong guidance. The recent pullback is a good buying opportunity to get into the industry for investors willing to stay put for the long run.

Semiconductors & Wafer Fab Equipment

The semiconductor industry was estimated worth $681 billion in 2024. A rapidly expanding industry that now plays at least some type of role for the future of almost all sectors.

Wafer fab equipment is crucial to the finalization of microchips through cleaning, etching, deposition, and photolithography. Intricate circuits are created through the equipment, resulting in completed microchips.

Capital expenditure on data centers is expected to reach $2.5 trillion over the next 5 years, which is anticipated to drive $200 billion in WFE investment. For LRCX, the demand will be there moving forward. It's the stiff competition that will create some challenge for them in gaining market share.

What Led to the Decline?

Revenue and profitability peaked in 2022 and 2023 for LRCX, with a decent dip in 2024 following. The stock price moved accordingly and began to slide after its July 10 peak. The price movements make sense as earnings declined for the stock through CY 2023 and the price kept climbing into the summer of 2024. LRCX P/E ratio tripled in this time. Lower earnings, higher valuation and less profitability, were not a track for success, and it finally caught up with them.

Back on Track

Calendar year revenue 2024 was $16.2 billion, as reported in their earnings in late January. Q1 2025 revenue and profitability continued the rebound in getting back on track. In fact, revenue, gross profit, operating income, and net income, have all increased in each of the last 6 quarters. CY 2024 earnings were $3.36, up from $2.73 in 2023.

CY 2024 earnings were $3.36, up from $2.73 in 2023. TTM cash from operations and free cash flow are also at all-time highs for the company, almost double that of FY2022. The dog days seem to be in the past for LRCX, as much of the data is showing positive signs across the income statement and cash flow.

Long Run Growth and Guidance

You can see the ebbs and flows in the company's revenue, including the big drop-off through 2023 that I have already alluded to. Over a decade, they have always responded and have seen tremendous growth in that span. As revenue has grown, operating income percentage has increased over time and has consistently been above 30% for a few years.

In their earnings call next week, it's expected they will report an EPS of $1, up 28% YoY, and predicted revenue of $4.64 billion, 22% higher than the same quarter last year. Their three-year guidance is shown above, and they are hopeful of revenue 60% higher by CY 2028, and operating margin to continue climbing into the mid-30s by percentage.

The Tariff Effect

LRCX is a global company, so of course the recent tariff changes will have an effect on the company. They are part of the global supply chain as they have manufacturing globally and see revenue from many continents. In the most recent quarter, China accounted for the largest chunk of their revenue at 31%. It has been reported that $700 million in sales from restricted Chinese customers, will not materialize in 2025, which will take a hit on their sales.

The good news for LRCX is that they have 6 manufacturing plants in the United States, 5 in Asia, and 1 in Europe, so they are spread well. From April 2 to April 4, the stock fell from about $73 per share to $59, as the U.S. and China went back and forth on increasing tariffs. A difficult tariff landscape can present challenges for LRCX, but that price drop presents a better buying opportunity, and they are still suited fine with their localized manufacturing in the U.S. and in Asia.

Peers

The WFE industry has performed well over the 5 years as tech and semiconductors have become a focus or need across so many other sectors. Among their peers, LRCX has performed in the middle, slightly trailing Applied Materials (AMAT), but largely trailing KLA Corp. (KLAC).

Also, seen below is a valuation comparison among a few competitors. LRCX is under the average in all categories. KLAC, the outperformer of the group, has almost a 1.5x EV/Sales and a 25% higher P/E than LRCX. I think over the long haul, this industry is a good place to be, and the low valuation with recent upgrades and increased guidance, leads me to like LRCX.

Conclusion

Looking back at the data, the declining earnings, higher valuation, and lowering profitability through 2023, the stock was due for a pullback. The markets have been volatile for other reasons, but I think this was fair for LRCX stock to come down to earth.

I think it's the right time to get back in and wait it out. Revenue, earnings, profitability, and cash flow are all on the rise in the last year. Tariffs pose a challenge, but with localized manufacturing and a strong U.S. presence, I think they are in a decent position compared to some companies and industries. LRCX is a pacesetter in an industry that has massive growth potential. Buy this stock and hold for a while.

data.title: Xunlei Limited Has What It Takes To Bounce Back
data.description:
Xunlei Limited (NASDAQ:XNET), a provider of digital entertainment, cloud computing and distributed cloud services in China, was by no means alone in getting whacked, but the stock has nonetheless been adversely affected by the recent selloff in stocks, which came in the wake of trade tensions as a result of the imposition of tariffs in the U.S. However, while it may take some time for it to happen, there is reason to be optimistic in the ability of XNET to fully recover from the recent turbulence. Why will be covered next.

XNET seems to have found support

A previous article from last December noted how XNET’s presence in China presented a number of challenges for the company, something that turned out to be well warranted in hindsight given what has happened in the new year. On the other hand, XNET is not without its strong points either, including a number that appear to have flown under the radar. In the end, XNET was rated a buy.

The chart above shows how the stock rallied in early 2025, which eventually resulted in a new 52-weeks high of $5.52 per ADS as recently as March 11. However, XNET was forced to surrender a large portion of its substantial YTD gains after stock markets sold off when it became clear the U.S. was about to impose tariffs on other countries, including China where XNET operates, which is something that has the potential to evolve into a global trade war and everything else that comes along with it.

XNET lost almost half its market cap during the downdraft that followed when the stock hit an intraday low of $2.83 on April 9, although XNET closed the day higher at $3.19, thanks to a huge rally in the stock market after the U.S. announced a delay in tariffs. More significantly, it is worth noting how the stock has kept its distance from the $3 price point, ignoring intraday moves, by continually closing above this price level during the recent turmoil, even though there were several opportunities for this price level to be breached. The closest the stock got was on April 8 when XNET closed at $3.02. This does not appear to be a coincidence.

Notice how in the prior chart the stock went from a 52-weeks low of $1.46 to a 52-weeks high of $5.52. If we then assume the recent decline in the stock is a retracement of this move up, then the 61.8% Fibonacci retracement of $1.46 to $5.52 is $3.01, which is just $0.01 below $3.02, or as low as the stock was able to close in recent weeks. This suggests XNET has encountered support, which helps explain why the stock has been moving higher lately with XNET closing at $3.57 per ADS on April 14.

Why it is worth betting on long XNET

The tariff dispute overshadowed a lot of things, but it is worth mentioning that XNET released its latest quarterly results on March 13. The Q4 FY2024 report, though, was a mixed bag and the report did not move the needle much as far as the stock was concerned. FY2024 revenue, for instance, declined by 11.3% YoY to $323.1M and GAAP earnings declined from $0.22 per ADS in FY2023 to $0.02 per ADS in FY2024, neither of which are appetizing if you are a bull.

However, it needs mentioning that FY2024 GAAP net income of $0.7M or $0.02 per ADS was adversely impacted by a goodwill impairment charge of $20.7M in the last quarter of FY2024. In terms of non-GAAP, which strips out the impact, FY2024 earnings came in at $0.38 per ADS, or $0.01 more than in FY2023. XNET managed to grow the bottom line in terms of non-GAAP, even with lower revenue.

In terms of multiples, XNET is valued at 0.63x TTM sales of $323.1M and a market cap of $219.4M or $3.57 per ADS since the total number of ADS/ADRs is about 61.47M. XNET also has a P/E ratio of 9.6x with the stock at $3.57 per ADS and TTM EPADS of $0.38. In addition, while the top line decreased in FY2024, revenue seems to be recovering with Q4 FY2024 revenue up by 9.3% YoY. Furthermore, guidance calls for Q1 FY2025 revenue of $85-89M, an increase of 8.2% YoY, at the midpoint, compared to revenue of $80.4M in Q1 FY2024.

In comparison, FY2021 non-GAAP EPADS was $0.11, which means earnings grew at a CAGR of 51.17% in FY2021-2024, although admittedly from a low base. According to the methodology espoused by say Peter Lynch, XNET can be argued to be undervalued because XNET has a PEG ratio of 0.19, or well below one, using a P/E ratio of 9.6x and assuming EPADS continues to grow at a CAGR of 51.17% in the next three years. According to the same Peter Lynch, a fair value for XNET is around $19.44 with TTM EPADS of $0.38 and if we assume earnings continue to grow by 51.17%, on average, per year by extrapolating the earnings growth seen in recent years into the future.

The income statement is not alone in arguing that XNET is undervalued. The same can be said looking at the balance sheet. XNET has cash, cash equivalents and short-term investments totaling $287.5M or $4.68 per ADS, which exceeds XNET’s market cap of $219.4M or $3.57 per ADS. Granted, XNET has almost $30M of total debt to account for, but the balance sheet also includes another $30.6M in long-term investments, which essentially cancel each other out.

In all, XNET has a book value of $317.8M with total assets of $473.9M and total liabilities of $156.1M, which converts to a book value of $5.17 per ADS. XNET is thus valued at less than book value with the ADS at $3.57, which implies a price-to-book of 0.69x. This can also be used to argue XNET is undervalued. Combine all the above with apparent support in the charts, and there are a number of solid arguments in favor of long XNET.

Are there risks to long XNET?

Long XNET may have solid arguments in its favor, but there are also risks worth mentioning. As shown earlier, the stock took it on the chin after the market realized the U.S. and China were headed for a clash due to tariffs. XNET is not in the business of exporting goods to the U.S., so there should not be much of an immediate impact on XNET due to tariffs.

Where XNET could be impacted is if the Chinese economy slows down to the point Chinese consumers start to pull back on spending, including on services like the ones from XNET in areas such as entertainment. The big decline in the stock was therefore understandable from that standpoint, since there could be a potential fallout for XNET as a result of tariffs, even if it takes longer for XNET to feel the effects compared to those companies that export a lot of consumer goods to the U.S.

The adverse impact on the stock could continue for a while since the tariff dispute remains. While the U.S. has shown a willingness to engage in talks with other countries on the subject of tariffs, it is not at all certain whether the trade dispute between the U.S. and China can be resolved anytime soon. This suggests that those long XNET should be prepared for further headwinds as a consequence of the tariff dispute between the U.S. and China.

XNET is also listed in the U.S. as an ADS/ADR, which raises a few issues for shareholders. For once, ADRs are not ideal when it comes to protecting the rights of shareholders. Furthermore, the U.S. Treasury Secretary recently suggested in an interview that the delisting of Chinese stocks should not be ruled out, but an option that is on the table. This could impact XNET.

However, while the possibility of a delisting is a risk for XNET, it is arguably unlikely to happen. If XNET or other Chinese stocks are delisted, it will be the shareholders and not the company itself that will be most affected. True, companies will no longer be able to raise capital on U.S. exchanges, but they can always list on another non-U.S. exchange.

In contrast, shareholders, including many of whom are from the U.S., will be in limbo as they ponder the status of their investment. What could happen is that the delisted company lists shares on another exchange and offers shareholders the option of exchanging their old shares for new ones. Chinese companies would still be impacted, but nothing that really hobbles them.

In comparison, the U.S. would lose the ability to regulate and oversee these stocks since they will no longer fall under U.S. jurisdiction. Delisting Chinese stocks is arguably not a good move for the U.S. for these reasons and therefore unlikely, although there are no guarantees here. Keep in mind that the possibility of delisting stocks like XNET popped up before when there was a dispute between the U.S. and China regarding accounting rules, but this ultimately did not come to pass. This is likely to happen again.

Investor takeaways

XNET saw its share price collapse as a result of the stock market turbulence following the introduction of tariffs by the U.S. government. The stock lost almost half its value at one point, but the stock reversed course after the U.S. government paused most of the tariffs on other countries, with the notable exception of China.

XNET has nonetheless responded well to the less hawkish stance on tariffs, but it would be premature to say that the dispute about tariffs has been resolved. XNET still faces the possibility of further headwinds due to the uncertainty caused by tariffs on the global economy. Those long XNET should not be surprised if the stock is confronted with further downward pressure due to global trade disputes.

Nevertheless, there is a case to be made for long XNET. The large and rapid drop in the stock price may have caused consternation, but it also means the price to get in on XNET is much lower, especially in comparison to as recently as early March. XNET can also be argued to be undervalued at current levels using more than one commonly used methodology.

Both the income statement and the balance sheet point in the direction of XNET being undervalued. For instance, XNET is valued at significantly below book value with a price-to-book of 0.69x, and that is for a stock with a growing bottom line. Just the cash alone suggests XNET ought to trade higher than where it is at the moment. XNET has a PEG ratio well below one, which also suggests XNET is undervalued at current levels.

The charts also favor the bull case for XNET. The recent decline in the stock seems to have been halted with the aid of support stepping in. This suggests there are enough investors out there that are bullish enough on the stock to push XNET higher. If support is in place, then that further strengthens the bull case for XNET.

I am bullish on XNET with all the above in mind. The risk trade tensions pose to XNET is still out there, and it is possible the stock could suffer additional setbacks if stock markets come under renewed pressure due to a possible escalation in trade tensions. The lingering uncertainty as a result of tariffs makes long XNET unsuitable as a momentum play, since the stock could reverse course at any time if or when the trade dispute intensifies.

XNET looks better if longs are in it for the long run. Valuations are low, and arguably much too low. This gives XNET a good foundation from which to appreciate. The one caveat here is that one must be willing and able to withstand possible short-term setbacks by holding XNET for the long term. If this is not the case, long XNET may not work out due to the risk the stock fails to make much headway in the short term as a consequence of the uncertainty imposed by the unresolved tariff dispute.

Bottom line, long XNET has a number of strong arguments in its favor, but one has to accept the risk the stock could decline nonetheless because of the dispute regarding tariffs. If the risk is too much to bear, then XNET may not be for everyone, since the tariff issue is likely to stay around for some time. But if the risk is acceptable, then long XNET looks like a winning play, provided it is for the long term.

data.title: Uber: Undervalued Despite Its Profitable Transformation
data.description:
Introduction

Uber Technologies (NYSE:UBER) has transformed from just a ride-hailing service to a transportation and logistics conglomerate in just a matter of years. The following article uncovers what expectations of growth and profitability are implied by Uber’s current valuation and why I believe Uber is positioned to outperform.

Overview

Let’s get down to business. In 2024, Uber’s revenue mix by products were as follows:

  • Mobility: $25.087 billion (26% year-over-year growth)
  • Delivery: $13.75 billion (13%)
  • Freight: $5.141 billion (-2%)

The ride-sharing market (“Mobility”) is, essentially, a duopoly between Uber and Lyft (LYFT). Uber maintains a market share of >75% with Lyft accounting for the remaining share. Mobility is Uber’s most profitable segment, with revenue margins eclipsing 30% in 2024. This is a $42.9 billion market that is expected to grow at a CAGR of 13.7% in the next few years.

The food delivery market (“Delivery”) is highly fragmented. DoorDash is the market leader, commanding over 50% market share. Uber Eats holds a respectable share of approximately 25% and is gaining in urban areas like New York City. Grubhub comes in third. Multiple other companies struggle for > 3% market share. Food delivery is another multi-billion dollar market with a CAGR near 10% expected up to 2032.

Uber Freight (“Freight”) is Uber’s newest segment (2017). According to its website, Uber Freight “delivers an end-to-end enterprise suite of Relational Logistics to advance supply chains and move the world's goods.” This market is undergoing a significant digital transformation and Uber is emerging as a key player. Competitors here include C.H. Robinson and UPS (UPS). Notably, this segment remains unprofitable for Uber (adjusted EBITDA loss of $22 million) and experienced flat year-over-year growth in Q4 2024. This was attributed to lower revenue per load that was balanced by increased shipment volume.

Uber also generates revenue from advertising (recognized under “Delivery”) and its "Uber One" subscription, which reached 30 million global members at the end of 2024 (up 60% year-over-year). Uber has expanded globally (e.g. Latin America and Asia Pacific), with ex-U.S. revenues comprising almost 50% of total revenues.

Earnings

Financially, Uber booked its first profit in 2024. Revenue grew 18% year-over-year to $43.978 billion. Income from operations was $2.799 billion (up 152%). Free cash flow (FCF) reached $6.895 billion in 2024, representing a FCF margin of roughly 15.7%.

Here were Uber’s financial and operational highlights from Q4 2024:

Looking ahead to Q1 2025, Uber expects gross booking growth between 17% and 21% year-over-year, which represents steady growth. Adjusted EBITDA is expected to be between $1.79 billion and $1.89 billion, representing 30% to 37% year-over-year growth.

Financial Health

As of December 31, cash and cash equivalents were $5.893 billion. Total current assets were $12.245 billion, while total current liabilities were $11.476 billion (including $1.15 billion in current debt). Subsequently, UBER’s current ratio is over 1, which implies solid liquidity for the next twelve months. Uber does owe $8.347 billion in long-term debt. Total stockholders’ equity is $21.558 billion.

Valuation Assumptions

Next, I performed a discounted cash flow (DCF) analysis on UBER. My intention is to uncover what UBER's current valuation implies about growth and profitability expectations.

As for inputs, $50.35 billion represents consensus analyst expectations for 2025 (Year 1). Revenue growth of 15% represents an average over the nine-year projection period and is consistent with historical growth. The FCF margin of 15% represents the average over the projection period and is consistent with what Uber achieved in 2024. The beta of 1.32 (24-month) is from Seeking Alpha data. Beta differs depending on source and timeframe, but I thought 1.32 was a reasonable assumption. A terminal growth rate of 3% represents a standard modeling assumption and accounts for Uber's long-term growth potential. Uber’s cost of debt and tax rates are estimates related to its interest-bearing debt of $9.6 billion. Please note that the vast majority of Uber’s WACC discount rate is derived from equity (CAPM), so these estimates have very little impact on the final discount rate (10.48%).

Based on assumptions of 15% revenue growth and FCF margins, UBER appears undervalued by 28.58%. Again, my main interest is finding out what assumptions are “baked” into UBER’s valuation. My inputs are based on what, I believe, can be feasibly achieved. Valuation is highly subjective, so I do not concern myself too much with whether or not a stock appears "overvalued" or "undervalued" (unless it is "gross").

Judging from my sensitivity analysis, which also acts as a “reverse DCF,” UBER appears priced for 10.7% revenue growth, assuming everything else stays the same.

I also performed a Monte Carlo simulation to account for variation from key inputs. This provides a more realistic and dynamic view of share price outcomes compared to my static DCF model.

So, UBER's valuation appears within reason (its current price falls between the 5th and 95th percentiles). It is not "grossly" undervalued.

It's also worth comparing UBER’s valuation to other methods, like multiples. While the company’s operations involve transportation and delivery, it is predominately a technology platform (software, internet) with low capital expenditures. In this light, UBER’s forward P/E of 30.57 (with 15% projected growth) appears favorable to the sector median forward P/E of 155.59 (with 22% projected growth). To be fair, there is a lot of nuance to UBER’s official classification. It doesn’t operate like your typical software company and is unlikely to achieve the FCF margins that some software companies can reach. However, for the purposes of my analysis, cash flows are the ultimate equalizer and don’t care what industry a company is associated with. So, no matter how you slice it, UBER appears reasonably valued, and an argument can be made that it is actually undervalued.

Opportunities and Risks

First, Uber’s platform business model has high operating leverage potential. This is playing out in its Mobility and Delivery segments, which saw expanding margins in 2024 compared to 2023. The company maintains a dominant position in both these segments. Outside of these segments, Uber’s revenue is diversified, spanning advertising, Uber One, and freight digitalization. Uber is a globally recognized brand that is front-and-center on many smartphones. Critically, Uber is positioned as a leader in the autonomous vehicle market, securing partnerships with key leaders like Waymo. By the way, “Waymo on Uber” just launched in Austin, with an Atlanta launch imminent.

On the other hand, there are some key risks to consider. Uber just became profitable, and uncertainty exists regarding its ability to remain profitable. Uber relies on gig labor, making it more challenging to ensure quality service and balance supply and demand. In this vein, its brand image is vulnerable (as evidenced by past controversies regarding passenger safety). Uber Freight remains an unprofitable segment and could continue to drag on overall profitability. Competition is fierce in all markets. A long-term existential threat is autonomous vehicle technology/AI/robots, which could disrupt Uber’s core operations. Uber is sensitive to macroeconomic headwinds. People won’t be as willing to pay for food delivery if the economy is in the toilet. Likewise, during trying times, a half mile urban stroll may become preferred over a $30 plus tip ride-share. Labor laws could change (independent contractor versus employee) and dramatically impact Uber’s cost structure and operational model. Despite California’s Prop 22 being upheld in 2024 (“that categorically classified app-based drivers as independent contractors”), future legislation challenges could arise and even minor changes can have big consequences for ride-share companies like Uber.

Conclusion

Due to its global presence in multiple markets, Uber is an incredibly complex company to evaluate. As this is my first article on UBER, a lot of what preceded this conclusion was a quick overview.

Here are some key takeaways from my analysis:

  • Per DCF analysis, UBER’s stock valuation appears attractive and priced for modest growth relative to the secular growth trends it is exposed to.
  • Uber maintains leadership positions, strengthened by network effects, in massive, growing markets like ride-sharing and food delivery.
  • Uber demonstrated a clear path to profitability and robust cash generation in 2024, which de-risks the stock compared to its earlier years.
  • Uber has multiple avenues for growth (e.g., freight digitalization, autonomous vehicles, advertising, Uber One).

For investors willing to brave risks like competition, technology advancements, and regulatory changes impacting the gig economy, UBER seems like a great “Buy” at these prices. However, the potential for large returns comes with large risks, so investors should keep a close eye on ongoing regulatory developments, macroeconomic conditions, and competitive threats that could derail Uber's growth.

data.title: Buy The Dip: 6%-Yielding Inflation Hedges Getting Way Too Cheap
data.description:
Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell recently said that the Trump administration's tariff policies are likely going to complicate the Fed's efforts to keep inflation down while also striving for full employment, as its dual mandate requires. This is because tariffs will likely increase the cost of a lot of imports, thereby driving up inflation, while also weighing on the economy due to the uncertainties and disruptions to normal supply chains. This in turn will complicate matters for the Fed because if inflation bounces back due to the tariffs, the Fed may be inclined to want to hike interest rates. However, if the economy is also weakening due to the disruptions caused by the tariffs, the Fed will not want to hike rates but instead cut them to prop up the economy. It will therefore likely be caught between a rock and a hard place when it comes to trying to fulfill its dual mandate.

With this in mind, now is a good time for investors to start thinking about playing defense while also ensuring your portfolio is adequately hedged against inflation. One obvious option is gold (GLD) and gold-related companies like Newmont Corporation (NEM). However, gold and NEM have both been on a tear recently, meaning that they are no longer the compelling values that they once were. On top of that, neither of them pay much, if any, yield. So for investors who prioritize passive income from dividends, they really are not ideal investment opportunities. Instead, investors can look for real asset inflation protection elsewhere while generating an attractive yield. In this article, I will discuss two options that currently yield about 6% each that are deeply undervalued after a recent sell-off.

Opportunity #1

The first option that they can turn to is the Gladstone Land Corporation (LAND), which is a farmland REIT that trades at a steep discount to its net asset value, despite trading at a premium to its NAV on average over the past five years. Additionally, due to the recent sell-off in its stock price, the yield has reached about 6%, which is far above its historical average.

Meanwhile, its underlying portfolio is well diversified by crop type and geography.

The company also has a good track record of sustaining and even growing its dividend over time, especially when compared to other farmland REITs like Farmland Partners (FPI).

While the underlying portfolio and balance sheet are quite solid with well-staggered debt (weighted average remaining term to maturity of 8.0 years) and lease (weighted average remaining lease term of 5.1 years) maturities, the company also has been reducing its cash flow burden by repurchasing its preferred stock at a deep discount to par value, thereby providing even better support for its common dividend.

Last but not least, insiders are well aligned with shareholders due to management owning 9% of the common stock.

As a result, investors can earn a very attractive current yield backed by a well-diversified portfolio of a very defensive and inflation-resistant asset class with well-aligned management and a solid balance sheet and lease expiration set up. Best of all, this is on sale at a 33% discount to private market values right now.

Opportunity #2

Another attractive option for investors to earn a high yield from an inflation hedge is via Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ). CNQ is an energy production stock that should serve as a good inflation hedge because energy has historically delivered the strongest real returns across assets whenever inflation has been high. On top of that, CNQ has well-diversified energy exposure as its 2025 budget production mix has 27% coming from natural gas, 26% from heavy oil, 11% from light oil and NGLs, and 36% from SCO.

CNQ also has a lengthy production profile with the most approved developed and total approved reserves among its Canadian energy peers with a 33-year total approved reserve life index and has the largest crude oil reserves as well as the largest natural gas reserves in Canada. Furthermore, when compared to global energy peers, it has the second-largest total reserves. CNQ also has some of the lowest-cost energy production assets in the world, which means that it should continue to generate free cash flow and pay out its dividend even at materially lower energy prices. This gives it significant durability through all sorts of economic cycles, which explains why it has grown its dividend for 25 straight years on top of substantial share purchases.

In addition, it has a very strong balance sheet with investment-grade credit ratings from both Moody's and S&P, and it has $4.7 billion in liquidity with a very reasonable debt to equity ratio of 1.1 times. When you add all that up together with its roughly 6% dividend yield, which is quite high compared to its historic average, and its very cheap price to net asset value of 0.79 times—despite trading at over 1 times net asset value on numerous occasions throughout its history and trading on average in line with its net asset value throughout its history—CNQ offers investors a very attractive combination of current yield, dividend growth, share buybacks, conservative balance sheet, a deep and high-quality low-cost production profile, and a significant return on investment.

Coming from a company that focuses on a commodity that has proven to be one of the best at inflation hedges, CNQ is a great buy-the-dip opportunity for inflation-averse investors.

Investor Takeaway
